items.title: Customer churn with age and monthly spend
items.scenario: A telecom dataset has `age` (18–80) and `monthly_spend` ($15–$250). A k-NN classifier without scaling predicts almost entirely from spend because Euclidean distance is dominated by dollar magnitude. After `StandardScaler` fit on the training fold, neighbors balance both signals.
items.explanation: Distance-based models weight each unit of difference equally in every dimension; rescaling makes one year of age comparable to one scaled unit of spend.

items.title: Housing price regression with Ridge
items.scenario: Features include `sqft` (500–4000) and `lot_acres` (0.1–2.0). Ridge penalty λ‖w‖² treats coefficients equally only when features are comparable; unscaled sqft gets a tiny coefficient while acres looks 'important'. StandardScaler before Ridge yields interpretable relative penalties.
items.explanation: L2 regularization shrinks all weights but unscaled features hide which inputs truly matter; scaling aligns penalty with feature contribution.

items.title: Neural net inputs bounded to [0, 1]
items.scenario: A small MLP for sensor readings expects inputs in [0, 1] for stable sigmoid activations. Engineers apply `MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))` on training sensors, then transform live streams with the same min/max from install-time calibration data.
items.explanation: MinMax gives hard bounds useful for activation ranges; train-only min/max must be frozen for inference.

items.title: Random forest on raw tabular features
items.scenario: A fraud team trains `RandomForestClassifier` on transaction count, amount, and hour-of-day without any scaler. Tree splits on thresholds (`amount > 500`) work identically if amounts are multiplied by 1000, so the team skips scaling and focuses on encoding categoricals.
items.explanation: Monotonic rescaling does not change rank order, so tree split decisions stay the same—scaling is optional unless downstream steps need it.
